lists.title: Korean A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: First Korean: topic, subject, and object particles, the verb-last sentence, the copula, polite present and past, negation with 안, place particles 에/에서, question words, having with 있어요/없어요, counters, and linking with 하고/도.

lists.title: Korean A2 Grammar Path
lists.description: Getting things done: the 을 거예요 future, wanting with 고 싶어요, honorific requests with (으)세요, favours with 아/어 주세요, 못 vs 안, ability with (으)ㄹ 수 있어요, reasons with 아/어서, contrast with 지만, purpose with (으)러, and comparisons with 보다.

lists.title: Korean B1 Grammar Path
lists.description: Real sentences: the formal ㅂ니다 style, the ㅂ/ㄷ/르 irregular families, conditions with (으)면, command-friendly reasons with (으)니까, obligation and permission, noun-modifying clauses, ongoing and resulting states, and (으)ㄹ 때.

lists.title: Korean B2 Grammar Path
lists.description: Nuance: intention and inference with 겠, reported speech with 다고/라고, the 이히리기 passive and causative families, scene-setting with (으)ㄴ/는데, hedging with 것 같아요, and formal causes with 기 때문에.

lists.title: Korean C1 Grammar Path
lists.description: Register control: the full honorific machinery (께서, 시, humble 드리다/뵙다) and the plain written 한다-style of books and news.

lists.title: Korean C2 Grammar Path
lists.description: Native texture: squeezed quotations (대요/래요/냬요/재요) and the formal written connectives (으)며, (으)나, (으)므로.

points.title: Topic particle 은/는 (저는, 이것은)
points.function: Marks what the sentence is ABOUT: 는 after a vowel, 은 after a consonant
points.explanation: Korean tags the topic of a sentence with 은/는: 저는 학생이에요 (as for me, I'm a student). Use 는 when the word ends in a vowel (저는, 친구는) and 은 when it ends in a consonant (이것은, 선생님은). The topic is the frame — 'as for X' — and once set, you can drop it entirely in the next sentences.

references.title: How to Study Korean: Unit 1 particles

drills.translation: I am a student.
drills.hint: topic marker — 저 ends in a vowel

drills.translation: The teacher is Korean.
drills.hint: topic marker — 선생님 ends in a consonant

drills.translation: My friend is at home.
This is a book.
As for today, the weather is good.
drills.hint: topic marker — sets the frame 'as for today'

drills.translation: We study Korean.

points.title: Subject particle 이/가 (친구가, 물이)
points.function: Marks WHO or WHAT does it: 가 after a vowel, 이 after a consonant
points.explanation: 이/가 marks the grammatical subject — the one doing or being: 친구가 와요 (a friend is coming). 가 follows vowels (친구가), 이 follows consonants (물이). Against 은/는: use 이/가 for NEW information and answers to 'who?' (누가 왔어요? 친구가 왔어요), and 은/는 for what's already the topic.

references.title: How to Study Korean: 이/가 vs 은/는

drills.translation: A friend is coming.
drills.hint: subject marker — 친구 ends in a vowel

drills.translation: The water is cold.
drills.hint: subject marker — 물 ends in a consonant

drills.translation: The weather is good.
My little sibling is sleeping.
Who came?
drills.hint: subject marker — 'who' takes the subject form 누가

drills.translation: The coffee is delicious.

points.title: Object particle 을/를 and verb-last order (밥을 먹어요)
points.function: Marks the thing acted on; the verb always comes LAST
points.explanation: Korean sentences end with the verb: subject – object – verb. The object wears 을/를: 밥을 먹어요 (I eat rice — literally 'rice-OBJECT eat'). 를 follows vowels (커피를), 을 follows consonants (밥을). Because the particles say who does what, word order before the verb is flexible — but the verb stays at the end.

references.title: How to Study Korean: word order

drills.translation: I eat rice.
drills.hint: object marker — 밥 ends in a consonant

drills.translation: I drink coffee.
drills.hint: object marker — 커피 ends in a vowel

drills.translation: I read a book.
I study Korean.
I watch a movie.
I listen to music.

points.title: The copula 이에요/예요 (책이에요, 의사예요)
points.function: 'X is Y': 이에요 after a consonant, 예요 after a vowel
points.explanation: To say 'X is Y', attach the copula straight to the noun: 책이에요 (it's a book), 의사예요 (she's a doctor). 이에요 follows consonants, 예요 follows vowels. The negative is a separate word with 이/가: 학생이 아니에요 (is not a student).

drills.translation: This is a book.
I am a doctor.
That person is a teacher.
This place is a school.
I am not a student.
That is water.

points.title: Present polite 아요/어요 (가요, 먹어요)
points.function: The everyday polite ending: 아요 after bright vowels ㅏ/ㅗ, 어요 otherwise
points.explanation: Drop 다 from the dictionary form and add 아요 or 어요: 가다 → 가요 (go), 먹다 → 먹어요 (eat). If the stem's last vowel is ㅏ or ㅗ, use 아요 (살다 → 살아요); otherwise 어요 (읽다 → 읽어요). 하다 verbs become 해요 (공부하다 → 공부해요). This one form covers 'I go', 'she goes', and 'we go' — Korean verbs never change for person.

references.title: How to Study Korean: conjugation basics

drills.translation: I go to school.
My little sibling eats rice.
drills.hint: to eat (먹다) — stem vowel is not bright

drills.translation: My friend reads a book.
We study Korean.
I live in Seoul.
drills.hint: to live (살다) — stem vowel ㅏ is bright

drills.translation: My older sister drinks water.

points.title: Negation with 안 (안 가요)
points.function: Put 안 right before the verb: don't / doesn't
points.explanation: The simplest negative is 안 in front of the verb: 안 가요 (I don't go), 안 먹어요 (I don't eat). With 하다 verbs, 안 slips between the noun and 하다: 공부 안 해요 (I don't study). The longer twin 지 않아요 means the same and sounds a touch more formal: 가지 않아요.

drills.translation: I'm not going to school today.
drills.hint: the little 'not' word before the verb

drills.translation: My little sibling doesn't eat meat.
I don't drink coffee.
drills.hint: to drink — polite present after 안

drills.translation: I'm not studying today.
drills.hint: with 하다 verbs it goes between noun and 하다

drills.translation: My friend isn't going.
drills.hint: the longer negative: verb stem + 지 않아요

drills.translation: I don't watch television.

points.title: Place and time: 에 vs 에서 (집에, 집에서)
points.function: 에 = at/to (destination, time, existence); 에서 = where an action happens
points.explanation: 에 marks a destination or a time: 학교에 가요 (go TO school), 세 시에 (AT three o'clock), and where something simply exists: 집에 있어요 (be at home). 에서 marks where an ACTION happens: 집에서 공부해요 (study AT home). Rule of thumb: doing something somewhere → 에서; going somewhere or just being there → 에.

references.title: How to Study Korean: 에 and 에서

drills.translation: I go to school.
I study at the library.
My friend is at home.
I eat at a restaurant.
We meet at three o'clock.
I drink coffee at a cafe.

points.title: Past tense 았어요/었어요 (갔어요, 먹었어요)
points.function: Add 았/었 before 어요: bright-vowel stems take 았, the rest 었
points.explanation: The past slots 았/었 between stem and ending: 가다 → 갔어요 (went), 먹다 → 먹었어요 (ate). Bright vowels ㅏ/ㅗ take 았어요 (살았어요), everything else 었어요 (읽었어요), and 하다 becomes 했어요 (공부했어요). Same form for I/you/she/we — person never matters.

references.title: How to Study Korean: past tense

drills.translation: I went to school yesterday.
I ate rice in the morning.
I watched a movie on the weekend.
I studied Korean yesterday.
drills.hint: 공부하다 — 하다 becomes 했어요 in the past

drills.translation: I met a friend.
I drank water.

points.title: Question words: 뭐, 누구, 어디, 언제 (뭐 해요?)
points.function: Questions keep normal word order — just add the question word and a rising tone
points.explanation: Korean questions don't rearrange anything: 뭐 해요? (what are you doing?), 어디에 가요? (where are you going?). The polite ending 요 plus rising intonation is the whole question machinery. Who = 누구 (누가 as subject), what = 뭐/무엇, where = 어디, when = 언제, why = 왜, how = 어떻게.

references.title: How to Study Korean: question words

drills.translation: What are you doing now?
Where are you going?
Who is that person?
When did you come to Korea?
Why were you late?
How do you eat this?

points.title: 있어요/없어요: existing and having (시간이 있어요)
points.function: One pair covers 'there is / I have' and 'there isn't / I don't have'
points.explanation: 있어요 says something exists or you have it: 시간이 있어요 (I have time / there is time). 없어요 is its negative: 돈이 없어요 (I have no money). The thing possessed takes 이/가, not 을/를 — Korean literally says 'to me, time exists'. For people you respect, 있다 upgrades to 계시다: 할머니가 집에 계세요.

references.title: How to Study Korean: 있다 and 없다

drills.translation: Do you have time today?
I have no money right now.
The cat is in the room.
I have a question.
There is no milk in the fridge.
I have two older brothers.

points.title: Counters: 개, 명, 마리 with native numbers (사과 두 개)
points.function: Count things as NOUN + number + counter, with the native numbers
points.explanation: Korean counts with measure words: 사과 두 개 (apples, two-item = two apples), 학생 세 명 (three students), 고양이 한 마리 (one cat). The pattern is noun + native number + counter. Before a counter, the first numbers shorten: 하나→한, 둘→두, 셋→세, 넷→네. 개 counts objects, 명 people (분 politely), 마리 animals, 권 books, 잔 cups.

drills.translation: Two apples, please.
There are three students.
There is one cat.
I bought four books.
One cup of coffee, please.
I ate two apples.

points.title: Linking nouns: 하고 (and/with) and 도 (also)
points.function: 하고 joins nouns or means 'together with'; 도 replaces 은/는/이/가 to say 'too'
points.explanation: 하고 glues nouns together: 빵하고 우유 (bread and milk), and doubles as 'with': 친구하고 갔어요 (I went with a friend). Its formal twins are 와 (after vowels) and 과 (after consonants). 도 means 'also/too' and REPLACES the topic or subject particle instead of stacking on it: 저도 가요 (I'm going too — never 저는도).

references.title: How to Study Korean: 도, 하고

drills.translation: I bought bread and milk.
I watched a movie with a friend.
I want to go too.
I like coffee and tea.
Please give me a book and a notebook.
My little sibling learns Korean too.

points.title: Future with (으)ㄹ 거예요 (갈 거예요)
points.function: Plans and predictions: stem + ㄹ/을 거예요
points.explanation: The everyday future: 내일 갈 거예요 (I'll go tomorrow). Vowel stems take ㄹ 거예요 (가다 → 갈 거예요), consonant stems take 을 거예요 (먹다 → 먹을 거예요). It covers both plans (I'm going to…) and guesses about the present: 지금 집에 있을 거예요 (he's probably home now).

references.title: How to Study Korean: future tense

drills.translation: I will go to school tomorrow.
drills.hint: to go (가다) — future stem before 거예요

drills.translation: I will eat dinner in the evening.
drills.hint: to eat (먹다) — consonant stem takes 을

drills.translation: I will watch a movie on the weekend.
drills.hint: the future ending after the ㄹ stem

drills.translation: I will learn Korean next year.
My friend is probably home now.
drills.hint: 있다 as a guess about the present

drills.translation: I will go to Busan next week.

points.title: Wanting: 고 싶어요 (가고 싶어요)
points.function: stem + 고 싶어요 = want to; third person uses 고 싶어해요
points.explanation: Attach 고 싶어요 to any verb stem: 한국에 가고 싶어요 (I want to go to Korea), 쉬고 싶어요 (I want to rest). The stem never changes — no vowel matching. For what SOMEONE ELSE wants, Korean switches to 고 싶어해요: 동생이 자고 싶어해요 (my sibling wants to sleep).

references.title: How to Study Korean: 고 싶다

drills.translation: I want to go to Korea.
I want to eat kimchi.
Today I want to rest at home.
I want to speak Korean well.
My little sibling wants to sleep.
drills.hint: someone ELSE's wish uses a different ending

drills.translation: I want to meet my friend.

points.title: Polite requests and honorific (으)세요 (앉으세요)
points.function: One ending, two jobs: honoring the subject, and gentle commands
points.explanation: (으)세요 does double duty. As a request: 앉으세요 (please sit), 여기 보세요 (look here). As an honorific statement about someone respected: 할머니가 주무세요 (grandmother is sleeping — 자다 upgrades to 주무시다). Vowel stems take 세요 (가세요), consonant stems 으세요 (앉으세요). Some verbs swap entirely: 먹다 → 드세요, 자다 → 주무세요, 있다 → 계세요.

drills.translation: Please sit here.
drills.hint: to sit (앉다) — polite request, consonant stem

drills.translation: Goodbye (go peacefully).
drills.hint: to go — polite request, vowel stem

drills.translation: Enjoy your meal (eat well).
drills.hint: 먹다 has a special honorific verb

drills.translation: Grandmother is sleeping now.
drills.hint: 자다 has a special honorific verb

drills.translation: The teacher is in the classroom.
Please speak slowly.

points.title: Do it for me: 아/어 주세요 (도와주세요)
points.function: Verb + 아/어 주세요 asks someone to do something FOR you
points.explanation: 주다 (to give) after another verb turns it into a favour: 문을 열어 주세요 (please open the door — literally 'open-give'). Bright stems take 아 주세요 (사 주세요 — buy for me), the rest 어 주세요 (읽어 주세요 — read for me). Statements work too: 친구가 도와줬어요 (a friend helped me out).

references.title: How to Study Korean: 주다 favours

drills.translation: Please open the door (for me).
Please say it one more time.
Please take a photo (for me).
My friend helped me with homework.
drills.hint: 돕다 + 주다 in the past — did it for me

drills.translation: Please take a look at this.
Please go slowly (for me).

points.title: Can't: 못 vs won't: 안 (못 가요)
points.function: 안 = choose not to; 못 = unable to
points.explanation: 안 가요 means I'm not going (my choice); 못 가요 means I CAN'T go (something stops me). 못 sits right before the verb, and with 하다 verbs slips inside: 수영 못 해요 (I can't swim). The longer form is 지 못해요: 먹지 못해요. Pick by asking: is it refusal (안) or inability (못)?

drills.translation: I hurt my leg, so I can't go.
I can't swim.
I can't eat spicy things.
Today I'm deliberately not going.
It's noisy, so I can't sleep.
drills.hint: the longer inability form after 지

drills.translation: I couldn't do my homework yesterday.

points.title: Ability: (으)ㄹ 수 있어요/없어요 (할 수 있어요)
points.function: stem + ㄹ/을 수 있어요 = can; 없어요 = cannot
points.explanation: The full 'can' pattern: 한국어를 할 수 있어요 (I can speak Korean). Build the same stem as the future (가다 → 갈, 먹다 → 먹을), add 수 있어요 for can and 수 없어요 for cannot. 못 and (으)ㄹ 수 없어요 overlap — 못 is shorter and more spoken, this one is clearer and works in writing.

drills.translation: I can speak Korean.
drills.hint: 하다 — the same stem shape as the future

drills.translation: I can eat spicy food.
I can't meet today.
I can play the piano.
You can't take photos here.
Can you ride a bicycle?

points.title: Because / and then: 아서/어서 (배가 고파서)
points.function: Links a reason or a sequence; never takes past tense before it
points.explanation: 아서/어서 chains clauses two ways. Reason: 배가 고파서 밥을 먹었어요 (I was hungry, so I ate). Sequence where the first step leads into the second: 친구를 만나서 영화를 봤어요 (I met a friend and [with them] watched a movie). The tense lives at the END only — never 갔어서. It can't order anyone around: no commands after 어서 (that's 으니까's job).

drills.translation: I was hungry, so I ate.
It rained, so I stayed home.
I met a friend and (then, together) drank coffee.
I was tired, so I slept early.
drills.hint: 피곤하다 — the 하다 form of the connector

drills.translation: Sorry for being late.
drills.hint: 늦다 + the reason connector — tense never goes before it

drills.translation: I went to the market and bought fruit.

points.title: But and and: 지만, 고 (춥지만, 먹고)
points.function: 지만 = but; 고 = and (between clauses); both attach straight to the stem
points.explanation: 지만 contrasts: 춥지만 갈 거예요 (it's cold, but I'll go). 고 simply lists or sequences: 밥을 먹고 커피를 마셔요 (I eat, and [then] drink coffee). Both glue onto the plain stem of any verb — no vowel matching. Unlike 어서, 고 allows past before it in lists: 어제는 일했고 오늘은 쉬어요.

references.title: How to Study Korean: 고 and 지만

drills.translation: The weather is cold, but I will go.
I eat, and then drink coffee.
Korean is difficult but fun.
I did my homework and slept.
It was expensive, but I bought it.
In the morning I exercise and shower.

points.title: Going somewhere to do: (으)러 가요 (먹으러 가요)
points.function: stem + (으)러 + a motion verb = go/come in order to
points.explanation: For purpose plus movement: 밥을 먹으러 가요 (I'm going [somewhere] to eat), 친구를 만나러 왔어요 (I came to meet a friend). Vowel stems take 러 (보러), consonant stems 으러 (먹으러). It ONLY works with motion verbs like 가다/오다/다니다 — for other purposes Korean uses (으)려고.

drills.translation: I'm going (out) to eat.
I'm going to see a movie.
I came to meet a friend.
I'm going to the library to borrow a book.
I attend school to learn Korean.
I'm going to a cafe to drink coffee.

points.title: Comparing: 보다 and 제일/가장 (더 커요)
points.function: X보다 (더) = more than X; 제일/가장 = the most
points.explanation: Comparison hangs 보다 on the loser: 형은 저보다 커요 (my brother is taller than me). 더 (more) is optional but common: 저보다 더 커요. For superlatives put 제일 or 가장 before the describing word: 제일 좋아요 (like it the most), 가장 큰 도시 (the biggest city). Less = 덜.

drills.translation: My older brother is taller than me.
drills.hint: 'than' — attaches to the one being beaten

drills.translation: Today is hotter than yesterday.
I like summer the most.
Seoul is the biggest city in Korea.
The subway is faster than the bus.
This restaurant is less expensive than that one.

points.title: Formal style ㅂ니다/습니다 (감사합니다)
points.function: News-and-business polite: ㅂ니다 after vowels, 습니다 after consonants
points.explanation: The formal register of announcements, presentations, and shops: 감사합니다 (thank you), 알겠습니다 (understood). Vowel stems fuse ㅂ into the last syllable + 니다 (가다 → 갑니다), consonant stems add 습니다 (먹다 → 먹습니다). Questions swap 다 for 까: 어디에 갑니까? Everyday conversation stays in 요-form; this style signals distance and respect.

references.title: How to Study Korean: formal endings

drills.translation: Thank you very much.
I go to the office every day.
drills.hint: 가다 — the ㅂ fuses into the stem

drills.translation: I eat breakfast.
Yes, understood.
drills.hint: 알다 + 겠 in the formal style — the set phrase

drills.translation: Where are you going? (formal)
We will now begin the meeting.
drills.hint: 하다 + 겠 (intention) in the formal style

points.title: Irregular verbs: ㅂ, ㄷ, 르 (추워요, 들어요, 몰라요)
points.function: Three stem families that transform before vowel endings
points.explanation: Some stems shapeshift before 아/어. ㅂ-irregulars melt ㅂ into 우: 춥다 → 추워요 (cold), 돕다 → 도와요 (help). ㄷ-irregulars soften ㄷ to ㄹ: 듣다 → 들어요 (listen), 걷다 → 걸어요 (walk). 르-irregulars double the ㄹ: 모르다 → 몰라요 (not know), 빠르다 → 빨라요 (fast). Before consonant endings they behave: 춥지만, 듣고, 모르지만.

drills.translation: The weather is really cold today.
drills.hint: 춥다 — the ㅂ melts into 우

drills.translation: I listen to music.
drills.hint: 듣다 — the ㄷ softens to ㄹ

drills.translation: I don't know the answer.
My friend helps me.
drills.hint: 돕다 — the ㅂ melts into 오/와

drills.translation: The subway is faster than the bus.
I walk in the park every day.
drills.hint: 걷다 — the ㄷ softens to ㄹ

points.title: If and when: (으)면 (시간이 있으면)
points.function: stem + 면/으면 covers both 'if' and 'when(ever)'
points.explanation: One ending for conditions and habitual whens: 시간이 있으면 오세요 (come if you have time), 봄이 오면 꽃이 펴요 (when spring comes, flowers bloom). Vowel stems take 면 (가면), consonant stems 으면 (먹으면). 만약 up front flags a pure hypothesis: 만약 비가 오면… (if it should rain…).

drills.translation: Call me if you have time.
If it rains, I'll stay home.
If you press this button, the door opens.
When you arrive in Korea, contact me.
If I have a lot of money, I'll travel.
If you're tired, rest.

points.title: Because (commands allowed): (으)니까 (늦으니까)
points.function: The reason connector that CAN introduce commands and suggestions
points.explanation: (으)니까 gives a reason like 어서, with two differences: it happily precedes commands and let's-suggestions — 늦으니까 빨리 가세요 (it's late, so hurry) — and it can carry past tense: 어제 갔으니까 오늘은 안 가요 (since I went yesterday, I'm not going today). 어서 can do neither. For apologies and thanks, though, Korean insists on 어서: 늦어서 미안해요.

drills.translation: There's no time, so go quickly.
It's raining, so take an umbrella.
I'm busy today, so let's meet tomorrow.
Since I worked yesterday, I'm resting today.
It's dangerous, so be careful.
It's delicious, so give it a try.
drills.hint: the consonant-stem form of the connector

points.title: Must and may: 아/어야 해요, 아/어도 돼요 (가야 해요)
points.function: 야 해요 = have to; 도 돼요 = it's okay to; (으)면 안 돼요 = must not
points.explanation: Obligation: verb + 아/어야 해요 — 지금 가야 해요 (I have to go now). Permission: 아/어도 돼요 — 여기 앉아도 돼요? (may I sit here?). Prohibition flips to (으)면 안 돼요 — 여기서 담배를 피우면 안 돼요 (you must not smoke here). The three form the classroom-and-real-life triangle of rules.

references.title: How to Study Korean: obligation and permission

drills.translation: I have to go home now.
You have to take the medicine.
May I sit here?
Is it okay to open the window?
You must not take photos here.
drills.hint: the same verb, after 으면 안

drills.translation: I have to hand in the homework by tomorrow.
drills.hint: 내다 (to hand in) + the obligation connector

points.title: Describing nouns: 는/(으)ㄴ/(으)ㄹ (읽는 책)
points.function: Whole clauses become noun modifiers — Korean's relative clauses, always BEFORE the noun
points.explanation: 'The book I'm reading' turns around in Korean: 제가 읽는 책 (I-read book). Verbs take 는 for present (읽는 책), (으)ㄴ for past (읽은 책 — the book I read), (으)ㄹ for future (읽을 책 — the book I'll read). Descriptive verbs use (으)ㄴ for their plain present: 큰 집 (a big house), 예쁜 꽃 (a pretty flower). No word for 'who/which/that' — position does everything.

references.title: How to Study Korean: describing nouns

drills.translation: The book I'm reading now is interesting.
drills.hint: 읽다 as a present-tense noun describer

drills.translation: The movie I watched yesterday was sad.
drills.hint: 보다 as a past-tense noun describer

drills.translation: I'm preparing the food we'll eat tomorrow.
drills.hint: 먹다 as a future noun describer

drills.translation: I want to live in a big house.
There are many people who learn Korean.
drills.hint: 배우다 as a present describer — 'people who learn'

drills.translation: It's a cake that I made.
drills.hint: 만들다 as a past describer (the ㄹ drops)

points.title: Ongoing and resulting: 고 있어요 / 아·어 있어요 (자고 있어요)
points.function: 고 있어요 = in the middle of doing; 아/어 있어요 = in the state that resulted
points.explanation: 고 있어요 is the -ing of action in progress: 지금 밥을 먹고 있어요 (I'm eating right now). 아/어 있어요 is for the STATE left after an action: 문이 열려 있어요 (the door stands open), 앉아 있어요 (is seated). Honor the subject by upgrading 있다: 선생님이 기다리고 계세요.

references.title: How to Study Korean: 고 있다

drills.translation: I'm eating right now.
My little sibling is sleeping now.
The door is (standing) open.
My friend is seated on the chair.
drills.hint: 앉다 — the state after sitting down

drills.translation: I am learning Korean.
The teacher is waiting.

points.title: When: (으)ㄹ 때 (먹을 때, 어렸을 때)
points.function: stem + ㄹ/을 때 = at the time of doing/being
points.explanation: 때 (time) after the future-modifier stem means 'when': 밥을 먹을 때 (when eating), 학교에 갈 때 (when going to school). For a finished past, 았/었을 때: 어렸을 때 (when I was young), 한국에 살았을 때 (when I lived in Korea). Nouns take 때 directly: 시험 때 (at exam time), 방학 때 (during vacation).

drills.translation: I watch TV when I eat.
drills.hint: 먹다 in the shape that goes before 때

drills.translation: When I go to school, I take the bus.
drills.hint: 가다 in the shape that goes before 때

drills.translation: When I was young, I lived in Busan.
When I have time, I read books.
drills.hint: 있다 in the shape that goes before 때

drills.translation: At exam time, I drink a lot of coffee.
drills.hint: nouns take the time word directly

drills.translation: When I lived in Korea, I learned Korean.

points.title: Intention and guessing with 겠 (하겠습니다, 맛있겠다)
points.function: First person: I will (resolve); elsewhere: must be / looks like
points.explanation: 겠 slides in before the ending and changes meaning by person. On yourself it's commitment: 제가 하겠습니다 (I'll do it). About anything else it's an on-the-spot inference: 맛있겠어요 (that looks delicious), 힘들겠어요 (that must be tough), 내일은 춥겠습니다 (tomorrow will be cold — the weather-forecast future). Set phrases live here too: 알겠습니다, 모르겠어요.

drills.translation: I will do it.
Wow, that looks really delicious!
I'm not sure. (I wouldn't know.)
drills.hint: 모르다 + the softening 겠 — the set phrase

drills.translation: Tomorrow will be very cold. (forecast style)
drills.hint: 춥다 + 겠 in the formal style

drills.translation: Moving alone must be tough.
I'll be going first. (taking my leave)

points.title: Reported speech: 다고/라고 해요 (온다고 해요)
points.function: Fold what someone said into your sentence with 다고 (verbs) / 라고 (nouns)
points.explanation: To relay speech, quote the PLAIN form + 고 하다: 친구가 내일 온다고 해요 (my friend says he's coming tomorrow). Action verbs in present take ㄴ/는다고 (간다고, 먹는다고), descriptive verbs keep 다고 (바쁘다고), nouns take (이)라고 (학생이라고 해요 — says he's a student). Past adds 았/었다고. It's also how Korean names things: 이것을 김치라고 해요 (this is called kimchi).

drills.translation: My friend says he's coming tomorrow.
drills.hint: 오다 in the quoted plain form + 고

drills.translation: My sibling says she's busy now.
drills.hint: descriptive verbs keep plain 다 + 고

drills.translation: They say that person is a doctor.
drills.hint: nouns take their own quote particle

drills.translation: In Korean, this is called kimchi.
drills.hint: naming uses the noun quote particle

drills.translation: My friend said she watched a movie yesterday.
drills.hint: past inside the quote: 보다 → 봤 + the quote ending

drills.translation: The teacher said there is homework.

points.title: Passive and causative: 이히리기 (열리다, 먹이다)
points.function: One suffix family, two directions: be-done-to (passive) and make-do (causative)
points.explanation: Korean derives both voices with the 이/히/리/기 suffixes — which verb takes which is memorized, not computed. Passive: 열다 → 열리다 (be opened: 문이 열렸어요), 듣다 → 들리다 (be heard: 소리가 들려요), 보다 → 보이다 (be visible: 산이 보여요). Causative: 먹다 → 먹이다 (feed), 입다 → 입히다 (dress someone), 자다 → 재우다 (put to sleep). The catch-all causative is 게 하다: 웃게 해요 (makes me laugh).

references.title: How to Study Korean: passive verbs

drills.translation: The door was opened by the wind.
drills.hint: 열다 in its passive form, past

drills.translation: The sea is clearly visible from here.
Music can be heard from the next room.
The mother feeds the baby.
drills.hint: 먹다 in its causative form — make/let eat

drills.translation: She dresses the child.
That movie always makes me laugh.

points.title: Background and contrast: (으)ㄴ/는데 (바쁜데, 가는데)
points.function: Sets the scene, contrasts, or softens — Korean's favourite clause glue
points.explanation: 은데/는데 frames what follows: 지금 바쁜데 나중에 전화해도 돼요? (I'm busy right now — can I call later?). Action verbs take 는데 (가는데, 먹는데), descriptive verbs (으)ㄴ데 (바쁜데, 추운데), nouns 인데. It contrasts (형은 큰데 저는 작아요), introduces background before a request, and trails off politely on its own: 좀 비싼데요… (it's a bit pricey…).

drills.translation: I'm busy now — can I call you later?
It's raining outside — do you have an umbrella?
My older brother is tall, but I am short.
I live in Korea, but I can't speak Korean.
drills.hint: 살다 + the contrast connector (the ㄹ drops)

drills.translation: I'm a student, so (as background) I have no money.
drills.hint: the noun form of the connector

drills.translation: This is a bit expensive… (trailing off)

points.title: Seems like: 것 같아요 (비가 올 것 같아요)
points.function: Hedge anything: modifier form + 것 같아요 = it seems / I think
points.explanation: Korean softens claims with 것 같아요 after the noun-modifier forms: 비가 올 것 같아요 (it looks like rain — future ㄹ), 자는 것 같아요 (he seems to be sleeping — present 는), 벌써 간 것 같아요 (seems she already left — past ㄴ). Koreans also use it for polite opinions where English would be direct: 좀 매운 것 같아요 (I find it a bit spicy).

references.title: How to Study Korean: 것 같다

drills.translation: It looks like it's going to rain.
drills.hint: 오다 in the future modifier shape

drills.translation: My sibling seems to be sleeping now.
drills.hint: 자다 in the present modifier shape

drills.translation: It seems my friend already left.
drills.hint: 가다 in the past modifier shape

drills.translation: I find this food a bit spicy.
drills.hint: 맵다 in its modifier shape (the ㅂ melts)

drills.translation: I think that movie will be fun.
It seems the roads are jammed.
drills.hint: 막히다 in the past/state modifier shape

points.title: Because, formally: 기 때문에 (일 때문에)
points.function: Noun/stem + 때문에 pins the cause; heavier than 아서 or 으니까
points.explanation: 때문에 hangs off a noun (일 때문에 못 가요 — I can't go because of work) or a nominalized verb with 기 (비가 오기 때문에 — because it is raining). It states causes with written-register weight; conversation prefers 어서/으니까. Blame or credit a WHOLE clause with 았기/었기 때문에 for past causes. Related: 덕분에 (thanks to) for good causes, 때문에 alone can sound accusatory.

references.title: How to Study Korean: 기 때문에

drills.translation: I can't go because of work.
drills.hint: the 'because of' word after a noun

drills.translation: The match was cancelled because it is raining.
I have to study because there's an exam.
Thanks to my friend, I came to Korea.
I'm tired because I slept late.
Because it snowed a lot, the roads are slippery.

points.title: Honorific machinery: 께서, 드리다, 뵙다 (선생님께서 말씀하셨어요)
points.function: Raising the subject (께서 + 시) and lowering yourself (humble verbs)
points.explanation: Full honorific Korean works both directions. Raise the respected subject: 이/가 becomes 께서, 에게 becomes 께, and the verb takes 시 — 선생님께서 말씀하셨어요 (the teacher spoke). Lower yourself with humble verbs: 주다 → 드리다 (give TO a superior), 만나다 → 뵙다 (meet a superior), 말하다 → 말씀드리다, 묻다 → 여쭤보다. The choreography: honor THEM with 시, humble YOURSELF with 드리다-verbs — never 시 on your own actions.

drills.translation: The teacher has arrived.
Grandfather spoke.
drills.hint: 말하다 upgraded twice: 말씀 + 시, past

drills.translation: I gave a present to the teacher.
drills.hint: 주다 lowered to its humble form, past

drills.translation: I will meet the director tomorrow.
drills.hint: 만나다 lowered to its humble form + 겠

drills.translation: I called my parents. (humbly)
May I (humbly) ask you something?
drills.hint: 묻다 lowered to its humble form + 아도

points.title: Plain written style: 한다/는다 (신문체)
points.function: The narration register of books, news, and diaries — no 요, no audience
points.explanation: Written Korean narrates in the plain style: action verbs take ㄴ다/는다 (간다, 먹는다), descriptive verbs and 있다 keep bare 다 (크다, 있다), past is 았다/었다 (갔다), and the copula is 이다/다. 한국은 사계절이 있다 (Korea has four seasons) — this is textbook-and-newspaper voice, also the intimate speech of close friends (반말). Learn to READ it early; it's every book you'll open.

drills.translation: Korea has four seasons. (written style)
drills.hint: 있다 stays bare in the plain style

drills.translation: He goes to the library every day. (written style)
drills.hint: 가다 in the plain present — ㄴ다

drills.translation: The child eats. (written style)
drills.hint: 먹다 in the plain present — 는다

drills.translation: It snowed heavily in Seoul yesterday. (news style)
Seoul is the capital of Korea. (written style)
drills.hint: the copula in the plain style

drills.translation: The winter sea is truly beautiful. (written style)
drills.hint: descriptive verbs stay bare in the plain style

points.title: Quote contractions: 대요, 래요, 냬요, 재요 (온대요)
points.function: Squeezed reported speech: statements 대요, commands 래요, questions 냬요, suggestions 재요
points.explanation: Spoken Korean compresses 다고 해요 into 대요: 내일 온대요 (they say he's coming tomorrow). Each speech act has its squeeze: statements ㄴ/는대요 (간대요), noun-statements (이)래요 (학생이래요), commands (으)래요 (오래요 — told me to come), questions 냬요 (가냬요 — asked if I'm going), suggestions 재요 (가재요 — suggested we go). Hearsay 다면서요? (I heard that…, right?) rides the same rails.

references.title: How to Study Korean: quoted contractions

drills.translation: My friend says he's coming tomorrow.
They say that person is the new teacher.
Mom says to come quickly.
My friend asks when I'm going.
My sibling suggests we watch a movie together.
I heard you're going to Korea? Congratulations!

points.title: Formal written connectives: (으)며, (으)나, 므로 (있으며, 크나)
points.function: The connectives of reports and essays: 며 = and, 나 = but, 므로 = therefore
points.explanation: Formal prose swaps the spoken connectives for tighter ones: (으)며 for 고 (서울은 수도이며 최대 도시이다 — Seoul is the capital and the largest city), (으)나 for 지만 (노력했으나 실패했다 — strove but failed), (으)므로 for 기 때문에 (증거가 없으므로 무죄이다 — therefore not guilty). 며 also marks simultaneity like 면서. You'll meet these in contracts, papers, and news — produce them only in writing.

drills.translation: Seoul is Korea's capital and its largest city.
(We) strove hard, but the result was not good.
As there is no evidence, (the accused) is not guilty.
That country has a long history and a rich culture.
(He) studies while listening to music.
As it is raining, the event will be held indoors.
